author: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

In this book Augustin refutes the second letter which Petilianus wrote to him after having seen the first of Augustin’s earlier books. This letter had been full of violent language; and Augustin rather shows that the arguments of Petilianus had been deficient and irrelevant, than brings forward arguments in support of his own statements.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Whilst we bear the testimony of God to this and the like effect against the vain speaking of men, we are forced to undergo bitter insults from the enemies of the glory of Christ.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let them say what they will, whilst He exhorts us, saying, "Blessed are they which are persecuted for righteousness’ sake: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Blessed are ye, when men shall revile you, and persecute you, and shall say all manner of evil against you falsely for my sake."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What He says in the first instance, "for righteousness’ sake," He has repeated in the words that He uses afterwards, "for my sake;" seeing that He "is made unto us wisdom, and righteousness, and sanctification, and redemption, that, according as it is written, He that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And when He says, "Rejoice, and be exceeding glad, for great is your reward in heaven," if I hold in a good conscience what is said "for righteousness’ sake," and "for my sake," whosoever willfully detracts from my reputation is against his will contributing to my reward.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For neither did He only instruct me by His word, without also confirming me by His example.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Follow the faith of the holy Scriptures, and you will find that Christ rose from the dead, ascended into heaven, sitteth at the right hand of the Father.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Follow the charges brought by His enemies, and you will presently believe that He was stolen from the sepulchre by His disciples.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why then should we, while defending His house to the best of the abilities given us by God, expect to meet with any other treatment from His enemies?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: "If they have called the Master of the house Beelzebub, how much more shall they call them of His household?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If, therefore, we suffer, we shall also reign with Him.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But if it be not only the wrath of the accuser that strikes the ear, but also the truth of the accusation that stings the conscience, what does it profit me if the whole world were to exalt me with perpetual praise?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: So neither the eulogy of him who praises has power to heal a guilty conscience, nor does the insult of him, who reviles wound the good conscience.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Nor, however, is your hope which is in the Lord deceived, even though we chance to be in secret what our enemies wish us to be thought; for you have not placed your hope in us, nor have you ever heard from us any doctrine of the kind.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: You therefore are safe, whatever we may be, who have learned to say, "I have trusted in the Lord; therefore I shall not slide;" and "In God have I put my trust: I will not be afraid what man can do unto me."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And to those who endeavor to lead you astray to the earthly heights of proud men, you know how to answer, "In the Lord put I my trust: how say ye to my soul, Flee as a bird to your mountain?"
